original_abstract:
This book explores the critical and often contentious question of how to effectively nurture the potential of highly capable and motivated young people within the landscape of higher education. Set against a backdrop of global competition and the shift toward knowledge-based economies, the text investigates the rising prominence of excellence initiatives across Europe. 

While the United States has a long-established tradition of collegiate honors programs, similar frameworks are only recently gaining significant traction in European institutions. Based on extensive exploratory research into special talent provisions for nearly four million students at 303 institutions across 11 countries—including the Benelux, Nordic, and German-speaking regions—this study provides the first comprehensive inventory of European honors education. 

Beyond mapping existing programs, the book analyzes the complex interplay of national cultures, egalitarian traditions, and institutional structures that either foster or hinder talent development. By placing these initiatives in their localized educational and political contexts, the work offers valuable insights for scholars, policymakers, and educators seeking to "break the academic lockstep" and provide more tailored, challenging opportunities for the next generation of global leaders.
humanized_abstract:
The book addresses the contentious issue of how to promote the development of gifted and talented young learners within the context of higher education. This is a global concern, as countries compete internationally to develop their human resources and to adapt to the changing demands of a knowledge economy. The book examines the growing number of excellence initiatives being implemented across Europe.

Special talent provisions in higher education within Europe: an inventory of current provisions This study is the first to create an inventory of current special talent provisions in higher education within Europe, drawing on a rich, qualitative research base consisting of exploratory surveys carried out in 11 European countries that together cover almost four million higher education students and 303 universities (Benelux, Nordic, and German-speaking countries). The findings are published in the European Studies in Education book Special talent provisions in higher education within Europe: an inventory of current provisions. In the US, it is common practice that universities offer students the option to opt into a college honours programme, and during the last two decades other countries have started to follow.

This book goes far beyond the traditional inventory of existing programmes by systematically investigating the interplay of national cultures, egalitarian conceptions of education and other structural factors influencing those programs and their possible effects on learning opportunities for talented children in different societal settings. By providing such nuanced information on the possibilities and constraints facing gifted and talented pupils within specific local educational, political and socio-economic settings, this volume should be highly relevant for researchers, policymakers and educators wishing to break the academic lockstep and offer the right challenge in an appropriate learning environment to the next generation of world leaders.
